retrieved_docs.content: Pseudoexfoliation syndrome: Deposits of white dandruff-like material on the anterior lens surface, iris, and anterior chamber structures. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Pseudoexfoliation Syndrome and Glaucoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This systemic disorder is typically clinically diagnosed during routine ophthalmic examination with slit-lamp visualization of white, flaky fibrillar (pseudoexfoliative) material on the pupillary margin of the iris and the anterior lens capsule. [4] This condition affects the eyes, particularly the lens and the trabecular meshwork, which are responsible for regulating the flow of fluid in the eye. [5] Small flakes of material, resembling dandruff, tend to accumulate on the surface of the lens, the iris, and the ciliary body. This accumulation can cause increased intraocular pressure (IOP) inside the eye, leading to glaucoma and other vision problems. PEX is more common in individuals over the age of 60 and is often associated with a higher risk of cataract development. While there is currently no cure for PEX, early diagnosis, and treatment can help prevent or slow the progression of vision loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous conditions can lead to elevated IOP, corneal haze, inflammation of the conjunctiva and the anterior segment, and similar signs and symptoms seen in patients with AAC. When evaluating a patient presenting with these manifestations, the following differential diagnosis should be considered: Allergic conjunctivitis Bacterial conjunctivitis (pink eye) Viral conjunctivitis Keratitis Episcleritis or scleritis Eye trauma Chemical injury Corneal ulcer Open-angle glaucoma Drug-induced glaucoma Malignant glaucoma Neovascular glaucoma Phacomorphic glaucoma Senile cataract (age-related cataract) Lens subluxation [50] Migraine headache [51] Cluster headache Suprachoroidal hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: Fuchs Endothelial Dystrophy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic angle-closure glaucoma can mimic FED, presenting with corneal edema and elevated IOP. Gonioscopy findings of closed angles, IOP exceeding 30 mm Hg, and glaucomatous optic neuropathy confirm the diagnosis. Acute endothelial toxicity results in rapid-onset corneal edema following intraocular surgery or chemical exposure. A history of recent intraocular procedures or exposure to toxic agents such as mitomycin-C or BAK is key to identifying this cause. Other diagnoses that must be considered include the following: Primary central corneal guttata Aphakic bullous keratopathy, especially with a history of cataract surgery Recurrent corneal erosions Chandler syndrome Posterior polymorphous dystrophy Congenital hereditary stromal dystrophy Toxic anterior segment syndrome Hassall-Henle bodies Herpetic disciform keratitis Pigment dispersion syndrome Anterior uveitis Interstitial keratitis Herpetic stromal keratitis

retrieved_docs.title: Cataract -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Subcapsular cataract: An anterior subcapsular cataract is seen below the anterior lens capsule and is associated with fibrous metaplasia of the lens epithelium. The posterior subcapsular cataract lies in front of the posterior capsule and has a granular appearance or plaque-like appearance on oblique slit lamp evaluation. Posterior subcapsular cataracts appear black and vacuolated on retroillumination. The vacuoles are swollen migratory epithelial cells (bladder or Wedl), which are the same as those seen in posterior capsular opacification. Since the posterior subcapsular opacity is located at the nodal point of the eye, it affects the vision profoundly. The patients usually complain of glare, halos, and photophobia. [6]

retrieved_docs.content: Anterior uveitis Keratitis Corneal abrasion Acute angle closure glaucoma Conjunctivitis Scleritis

retrieved_docs.title: Pigment Dispersion Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA)
retrieved_docs.content: OCTA depicts reduced vascular density, an excellent diagnostic predictor of glaucoma. OCTA is a sensitive marker for diagnosing, progressing, and monitoring glaucoma with visual field changes. A good diagnostic OCTA is possible only with a good quality scan when factors like cataract, corneal opacity, eye blinking, and other artifacts are eliminated. A good signal strength and scan centration are other factors influencing the results. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Pseudoexfoliation Syndrome and Glaucoma -- History and Physical -- Slit Lamp Examination
retrieved_docs.content: The characteristic finding in PEX is the visualization of white, flaky, dandruff-like PEX material along the pupillary margin and anterior lens capsule (See image of PEX deposits on anterior structures). The anterior lens capsule shows a central disc and peripheral ring of PEX material with a clear intermediate zone maintained by pupillary abrasion. The whitish double concentric ring that is seen on the anterior portion of the lens is probably due to the movement of the iris over the lens. The central disc may be absent in up to 20% of cases, and the peripheral zone may not be completely visualized without the aid of pharmacological dilation. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Amyloidosis -- History and Physical -- Orbital Amyloidosis
retrieved_docs.content: Conjunctival amyloidosis can affect healthy people of any age or gender and may cause nodular or diffuse deposits on the bulbar or tarsal conjunctiva. The tarsal amyloid deposits on the upper eyelids can lead to ptosis, while bulbar amyloidosis commonly affects the superior fornices. Amyloid deposits may be unilateral or bilateral and have a firm, waxy appearance with color varying from yellow, pink, or salmon patches. In some cases, recurrent subconjunctival hemorrhages may occur due to increased capillary fragility caused by amyloid deposits. Other conditions, such as lymphoproliferative deposits and conjunctival inflammatory granuloma, must be ruled out. Excision biopsy and a characteristic histopathology result can confirm the diagnosis. [16] Conjunctival amyloidosis may be an early indication of systemic amyloidosis; hence, systemic evaluation and follow-up are necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Pigment Dispersion Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Goniotomy
retrieved_docs.content: This is performed with a Kahook dual blade by removing the trabecular meshwork. A multicentric study on PAOG eyes with goniotomy and phacoemulsification reported a mean IOP alleviation of 26.2% and 50% reduction in antiglaucoma medications. The most common side effects were pain, capsular opacification, and IOP spike. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Amyloidosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the part of the eye affected, complications vary and include: Orbital or ocular adnexal amyloidosis. Ptosis, eyelid swelling, proptosis, lid or conjunctival masses, ophthalmoplegia, and paresis of accommodation. Amyloid deposition in trabecular meshwork can lead to glaucoma. Corneal amyloidosis. Keratoconjunctivitis sicca, corneal opacification, and severe visual loss. Vitreoretinal disorders. ATTR amyloidosis causes significant visual defects due to dense vitreous opacities. [2] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634

Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, a group of agents that produce pupil dilation. As mydriatic drugs, they are used in ophthalmic practice to achieve mydriasis for diagnostic examinations, therapeutic procedures, and the management of certain eye conditions, reflecting their shared pharmacologic role in facilitating ocular assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2651
retrieved_docs.content: Superficial injury refers to a minor form of trauma that affects the outer layers of tissue, and one specific manifestation of this category is the corneal abrasion known in Arabic as خدش القرنية. This condition represents a superficial injury to the cornea, characterized by a shallow loss of epithelial cells that can cause pain, tearing, and visual disturbance. Recognizing that خدش القرنية is a child concept of superficial injury helps clinicians classify it appropriately within the broader spectrum of minor ocular traumas and guides prompt, targeted management to promote rapid healing and prevent complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 5 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is used as a permissible descriptor in the context of pathophysiology. The pathophysiological processes that can be qualified include تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ (scleritis), نوبة (an acute attack or episode), تقرّح الدوالي (ulceration of varicose veins), and فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني (excess intra‑ocular pressure). These qualifiers help clinicians describe the underlying mechanisms of inflammatory scleral thickening, episodic disease exacerbations, variceal ulcer formation, and elevated intra‑ocular pressure that may predispose to glaucoma, thereby improving the precision of diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: Facilitation of intraocular circulation is linked to the concept زَرَق, and glaucoma is classified within زَرَق, indicating that زَرَق serves as a unifying category that connects the therapeutic goal of improving intraocular blood flow with the disease entity glaucoma. This relationship highlights the relevance of زَرَق in understanding and potentially managing glaucoma through strategies that enhance intraocular circulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Hemorrhagic choroidal manifestations are noted as choroidal hemorrhage and massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق). Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded. Collectively, these entities illustrate the intersection of systemic vascular disease with specific retinal and choroidal hemorrhagic findings, neovascular disorders, inflammatory and infectious processes, traumatic and postoperative sequelae, and degenerative changes, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1920
retrieved_docs.content: Betaxolol, metipranolol and timolol are all members of the pharmacologic class described as beta‑blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics. This class groups drugs that combine beta‑adrenergic antagonism with ocular‑lowering effects, and each of these agents is used to treat glaucoma by decreasing intra‑ocular pressure, often through reduction of aqueous humor production. Their shared classification highlights their common mechanism of action and therapeutic role in managing glaucomatous disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: Beta blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the drugs betaxolol and metipranolol. Both betaxolol and metipranolol are beta‑adrenergic antagonists that are employed as antiglaucoma agents and miotics, helping to lower intraocular pressure by decreasing aqueous humor production. As members of this same therapeutic group, they share similar mechanisms of action and are used in the management of ocular hypertension and glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, meaning they are pharmacologic agents used to dilate the pupil. As mydriatic drugs, they act on the iris muscles to produce pupil enlargement, which is valuable in ophthalmic examinations and certain therapeutic procedures. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and similar clinical applications in eye care.

Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.
